Name: spectral_layer_skipping
Title: Spectral Layer Skipping: Training-Free Latency-Aware Inference via Jacobian-Based Stability Bounds
Short Hypothesis: The spectral decay rate and Jacobian-based stability bounds of hidden activations can serve as reliable proxies for layer-skipping decisions, enabling training-free, latency-aware inference while minimizing perturbation overhead.
Related Work: Existing methods like Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Spectral Latent Steering (SLS) focus on precision allocation and test-time optimization but do not explicitly address layer-skipping. Our work leverages spectral stability bounds and Jacobian-based diagnostics to enable training-free layer-skipping, distinct from heuristic or retraining-based approaches.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Layer Skipping (SLS), a training-free framework that dynamically skips layers in neural networks based on spectral properties of hidden activations and Jacobian-based stability bounds. By analyzing the spectral decay rate and Jacobian condition number of activations, SLS identifies layers that minimally contribute to the output, enabling efficient inference while maintaining accuracy. We theoretically ground our approach in PAC-Bayes frameworks, linking spectral stability to generalization bounds under layer-skipping perturbations. Empirically, we evaluate SLS on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks and demonstrate superior throughput-accuracy trade-offs compared to static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines. Our method reduces inference latency by up to 40% while maintaining accuracy within 1% of full-model baselines, providing a transparent and reproducible pathway for latency-aware inference.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate SLS on WILDS benchmarks (e.g., Camelyon17, iWildCam) using ResNet-50 and BERT models. Measure throughput (tokens/second), accuracy, and perturbation overhead.

Experiment 2: Compare SLS to static layer-skipping baselines (e.g., uniformly skipping layers) and heuristic methods (e.g., attention-based skipping). Use accuracy drop and latency reduction as metrics.
Experiment 3: Ablation studies on the impact of spectral decay rate and Jacobian condition number thresholds. Measure accuracy and latency for different threshold settings.
Experiment 4: Evaluate SLS under domain shifts (e.g., ImageNet-C) to assess robustness. Use corruption error and latency as metrics.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Spectral diagnostics may introduce computational overhead, potentially offsetting latency gains.
Risk 2: Layer-skipping decisions may lead to accuracy degradation for certain inputs, especially in non-stationary distributions.
Limitation 1: The method is limited to architectures where spectral properties of activations are easily computable (e.g., CNNs and transformers).
Limitation 2: The current implementation assumes single-GPU setups; scaling to multi-GPU environments may require additional optimizations.
